title: Tallow cold-process soap
subtitle: A firm, long-lasting bar made with rendered beef tallow, coconut oil, and a small castor addition
excerpt: Tallow soap is the oldest cold-process soap in European tradition. Rendered beef fat gives a hard, white bar with excellent cleansing properties and a close, creamy lather. Combined with coconut oil for bubbly lather and castor oil for lather stability, this formula produces a long-lasting bar that is noticeably mild once fully cured.
sourceNotes: SAP values used: beef tallow 0.141, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat applied; lye discount 33% of oil weight. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), chapters on animal fats in cold-process soap and oil-property tables. Anne Bramson, Soap: Making It, Enjoying It (1972), historical tallow soap recipes and traditional formulas. USDA AOCS fatty acid database, tallow oleic/stearic/palmitic profile.

recipe.batchNotes: Run any change to oil weights through a lye calculator before scaling. Never adjust the lye figure proportionally without recalculating for the exact oil blend.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow at least 2 days in the mould before unmoulding, then a minimum of 4 weeks on a curing rack before use. Six weeks gives a noticeably harder bar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool, dark cupboard stored on an open rack. Discard any bar that develops orange oily spots (DOS, dreaded orange spots, a sign of rancid superfat) or a sharp paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical reaction between fat and lye (sodium hydroxide) that produces soap and glycerine. Each oil or fat has a SAP value: the grams of lye needed to fully saponify one gram of that fat.
The percentage of fat left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5% superfat, 5% of the fat weight remains as free oil, giving a mild and conditioning bar.
The point at which the soap batter thickens enough that a drizzle on the surface holds its shape briefly before sinking. The signal that the oils and lye solution have emulsified.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Turn out the loaf after 2 days. The soap should be firm. Cut into bars. Wear protective gloves for the first few days after cutting; the bar is still caustic until fully cured.

projectSchedule.title: Turn bars
projectSchedule.body: Flip each bar so the underside cures evenly. Tallow soap firms up quickly; bars should already feel hard to the touch.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on the inside of the forearm. The bar is ready to use from this point.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: A fully cured tallow bar is noticeably hard, bright white, and mild. Stores two years in a cool, dark place.

content.text: Tallow is rendered beef fat, and for most of soap-making history it was the primary soap fat across Europe. It produces a hard, white bar with a close, creamy lather and excellent cleansing properties. This formula uses 70% tallow for the hard base, 25% coconut oil for bubbly lather, and 5% castor oil to stabilise the foam. At 5%
superfat

and 33% lye discount, the finished bar is mild, conditioning, and will last considerably longer in the shower than a high-coconut-oil bar. Cure time is 4 to 6 weeks. Yield is 8 bars of around 120 g each.
Before starting, put on protective gloves and goggles. Lye handling comes before any mixing.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 70% of oil weight; rendered from suet, or purchased ready-rendered
25% of oil weight; melt gently if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat on this oil blend

content.text: Method
Mix the lye solution

Work in a well-ventilated space. Put on protective gloves and goggles before opening the lye container. Weigh the sodium hydroxide into a small container. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add the lye to the water in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never add water to lye. The solution heats to around 80°C and gives off steam; stand back as you stir. Set the pitcher in a safe place to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Prepare the fats

Weigh the tallow into a stainless-steel or HDPE bowl. If the tallow is firm, melt it gently on low heat until liquid, then let it cool slightly. Melt the coconut oil if solid and weigh into the same bowl. Add the castor oil. Allow the fat blend to cool to 38 to 42°C so the temperatures of the fat blend and lye solution are close before combining.
Combine and blend to trace
Pour the lye solution into the fat blend in a slow, steady stream. Use the stick blender in short bursts of 5 seconds, stirring between each, until the batter reaches light
trace
: the consistency of thin custard. Tallow batter traces at a moderate pace. Pour promptly once light trace is reached.
Pour and insulate
Pour the batter into the silicone loaf mould. Tap the mould once to settle the batter. Wrap in a towel and leave in a warm place for 24 to 48 hours. The heat of
saponification
drives the soap through a gel phase. Tallow soap often gels readily, producing a very white, slightly translucent bar after cutting.
Unmould, cut, and cure

After 2 days, turn out the loaf. Tallow soap firms up faster than high-olive-oil bars. Cut into bars using the wire cutter or a sharp knife. Wear protective gloves during cutting; the bars are still caustic. Arrange on a rack with space between each bar. Leave in a cool, dry room out of direct sunlight for 4 to 6 weeks, turning every two weeks. The bars whiten further as they cure and the lather refines.
Storage and shelf life

Store cured bars on an open soap dish or rack. A closed container traps moisture and shortens shelf life. In a cool dark cupboard, fully cured bars keep two years. Discard any bar that develops orange oily spots (DOS) or a sharp paint-like smell. Both indicate rancid superfat oil.
